questions.prompt: Under accrual basis accounting, when is revenue recognised?
choices: When cash is received
When an invoice is sent
When the service is performed or goods are delivered
When the customer's cheque clears the bank
questions.explanation: Accrual basis recognises revenue when EARNED (service performed or goods delivered), not when cash is received. This matches revenue to the period in which it was earned.

questions.prompt: A consulting firm completes a $10,000 project in December but receives payment in January. Under accrual accounting, in which month is revenue recognised?
choices: January only
December only
Split between December and January
Neither — it's revenue only when cash is received
questions.explanation: Revenue is recognised in December when earned (work completed). Cash collection happens in January. Accrual basis separates the timing of earning revenue from receiving payment.

questions.prompt: A company pays $24,000 for 12 months of insurance on 1 October. By 31 December, how much should be recorded as an expense?
choices: $0 — expense is recorded when paid
questions.explanation: From October 1 to December 31 is 3 months. Expense = $24,000 / 12 × 3 = $6,000. The remaining $18,000 stays as Prepaid Insurance (an asset).

questions.prompt: Employees earn $15,000 in December salaries but are paid in January. What is the December adjusting entry?
choices: Dr Cash $15,000, Cr Salary Expense $15,000
Dr Salary Expense $15,000, Cr Salaries Payable $15,000
Dr Salaries Payable $15,000, Cr Salary Expense $15,000
Dr Cash $15,000, Cr Salaries Payable $15,000
questions.explanation: December expense (debit) is matched to December period. Salaries Payable (liability) increases with credit. The January payment will debit Salaries Payable and credit Cash.

questions.prompt: A customer pays $6,000 on 1 November for 3 months of service (Nov-Jan). On 30 November, what journal entry is needed?
choices: Dr Unearned Revenue $2,000, Cr Service Revenue $2,000
No entry — revenue was already recorded in November
Dr Cash $2,000, Cr Service Revenue $2,000
Dr Service Revenue $2,000, Cr Unearned Revenue $2,000
questions.explanation: One month of service is earned in November ($6,000 / 3 = $2,000). This reduces unearned revenue (liability) by debit and increases revenue by credit. Remaining unearned = $4,000.

choices: Revenue must equal expenses each period
All accounts must have equal debits and credits
Expenses must be matched to the period in which they help generate revenue
All transactions must have matching debit and credit entries
questions.explanation: The matching principle ensures expenses are recorded in the same period as the revenue they helped generate. This gives an accurate profit figure for each period.

questions.prompt: A company performs $8,000 of work in December but has not yet invoiced the customer. What adjusting entry is needed?
choices: Dr Accounts Receivable $8,000, Cr Service Revenue $8,000
Dr Cash $8,000, Cr Service Revenue $8,000
Dr Service Revenue $8,000, Cr Accounts Receivable $8,000
No entry needed until the customer is invoiced
questions.explanation: Revenue is earned in December even though cash hasn't been received. This creates an asset (Accounts Receivable) and recognises revenue in the correct period.

questions.prompt: Unearned revenue is best classified as:
choices: An asset — it represents future cash to be received
A liability — it represents a future obligation to perform work
Equity — the customer has invested in the company
An expense — the company has incurred future costs
questions.explanation: Unearned revenue is a LIABILITY because the company owes the customer a service. Once the service is performed, it transfers from liability to revenue.

questions.prompt: On 1 September, a company pays $3,600 for 6 months of rent (Sept-Feb). By 31 December, what amount should remain as Prepaid Rent?
questions.explanation: $3,600 / 6 months = $600 per month. From September 1 to December 31 = 4 months used. Rent Expense = 4 × $600 = $2,400. Remaining Prepaid Rent (January–February = 2 months) = 2 × $600 = $1,200. The question asks what REMAINS as Prepaid Rent at 31 December, which is $1,200.

questions.prompt: Allowance for doubtful debts is recorded to:
choices: Reduce inventory to lower of cost or market
Estimate and record bad debt expense and reduce receivables to net realisable value
Accrue revenue that may not be collected
Record interest earned on accounts receivable
questions.explanation: Allowance for doubtful debts is a contra-asset account that reduces accounts receivable on the balance sheet and records an estimated bad debt expense on the income statement. This matches the expense to the period revenue was earned.

questions.prompt: On 1 November, a company receives $9,600 in advance for a 12-month maintenance contract (Nov to Oct). At 31 December (year end), what is the adjusting entry?
choices: Dr Unearned Revenue $1,600, Cr Revenue $1,600
Dr Revenue $1,600, Cr Unearned Revenue $1,600
Dr Cash $9,600, Cr Revenue $9,600
Dr Unearned Revenue $9,600, Cr Revenue $9,600
questions.explanation: From Nov 1 to Dec 31 = 2 months earned. Monthly rate = $9,600 / 12 = $800. Revenue earned = 2 × $800 = $1,600. Adjusting entry: Dr Unearned Revenue $1,600 (liability decreases) Cr Revenue $1,600 (income recognised). Remaining $8,000 stays as Unearned Revenue (liability) for the remaining 10 months.

questions.prompt: A consultancy provides $8,000 of services in December but will not invoice the client until January. What is the December 31 year-end adjusting entry?
choices: Dr Accounts Receivable $8,000, Cr Revenue $8,000
Dr Revenue $8,000, Cr Accounts Receivable $8,000
Dr Cash $8,000, Cr Revenue $8,000
No entry needed until cash is received
questions.explanation: Accrued revenue (also called accrued income) is revenue earned but not yet billed or collected. The adjusting entry creates an asset (Accounts Receivable / Accrued Revenue) for money owed and recognises the revenue in the period it was earned. Dr Accounts Receivable $8,000, Cr Revenue $8,000. When the invoice is paid in January, Dr Cash, Cr Accounts Receivable.
